name: PoloDB
description: PoloDB is an embedded document database written in Rust that implements a lightweight MongoDB. It stores data in BSON format over a RocksDB LSM-Tree storage engine, providing a MongoDB-like API with collections, documents, and transactions. PoloDB runs in-process with zero runtime dependencies beyond libc, making it highly portable across macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android. It supports both serde-based typed structs and raw BSON documents via the polodb_core::bson module, with explicit transaction control via Database::start_transaction.
format_on_disk: BSON documents over RocksDB LSM-Tree (single file)
core_strengths: MongoDB-like API with collections, documents, and find queries
BSON-native storage with polodb_core::bson re-export and serde support
Zero runtime dependencies beyond libc, highly portable across platforms
Explicit transaction control with auto-transaction fallback per operation
Single-file storage format, simple to deploy and back up
Cross-platform including iOS and Android via Rust cross-compilation
best_use_cases: Rust desktop apps needing MongoDB-like document storage without a server
Mobile apps (iOS/Android) requiring embedded document database
CLI tools and daemons that need schemaless JSON/BSON persistence
Local-first applications prototyping against a MongoDB-compatible API
Edge devices and IoT where minimal dependencies and cross-compilation matter
remarks: PoloDB uses RocksDB as its underlying storage engine via polodb-librocksdb-sys, which requires a C++ toolchain to compile from source. The RocksDB dependency can complicate cross-compilation for target platforms like iOS and Android, though pre-built binaries exist for common targets. The API is MongoDB-inspired but not wire-compatible — it is an embedded library, not a drop-in MongoDB replacement. BSON is the native document format; JSON must be serialized to BSON. write operations require serde::Serialize and read operations require serde::Deserialize on document types. Sled and other Rust-native backends are being explored to replace RocksDB in future versions.
